coredata.dc:title: Designing of biomass-derived carbon quantum dots@polyvinyl alcohol film with excellent fluorescent performance and pH-responsiveness for intelligent detection
coredata.dc:creator: Tao, Xingyu
Liao, Meng
Wu, Fengxia
Jiang, Yuheng
Sun, Jianping
Shi, Shaohong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted extensive attentions because of their unique luminescent properties and promising prospects in intelligent sensing and detection. However, the increasing environmental pollution caused by chemical substances for synthesizing illuminants limits their expansion in practical applications. Herein, we employed a facile and green synthesis strategy to extract biomass-derived CQDs from natural lignocellulose. The obtained CQD nanoparticles not only showed excellent fluorescent performance, but also posed desirable environmental-friendly characteristic. Moreover, to relieve the fluorescence quenching induced by CQDs aggregation, the well-dispersed CQDs@polyvinyl alcohol (CQDs@PVA) composite film with strong hydrogen-bond interaction was successfully fabricated. The resultant film exhibited high transparency (transmittance of 88%), excellent mechanical flexibility (tensile strength of 39.7 MPa and elongation at break of 453%), and exceptional fluorescence performance. The high concentrated emitting centered in 502 nm emission wavelength was realized in a wide excitation range of 350 ∼ 420 nm with high-stability turquoise fluorescence. More impressively, this fluorescent film demonstrated a sensitive pH-responsiveness, i.e., the fluorescent intensity varied upon pH stimulation. As a proof-of-concept, an intelligent pH-detector based on this fluorescent film was rationally designed for real-time sensing and detection of pH variation in human sweat during movement processing. From fundamental materials to desired applications, this study not only enriches the synthetic strategy of illuminants in an environment-friendly manner, but also paves the way for further applications in wearable, real-time health monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass-derived carbon quantum dots
Polyvinyl alcohol
Fluorescence
pH-responsive
Wearable intelligent detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphologies and fluorescence properties of CQDs@PVA film
Wearable pH-detector assembled with CQDs@PVA film
Preparation of CQD nanoparticles and CQDs@PVA film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the preparation procedure of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and CQDs@PVA film (a); SEM images of wood chips (b) and lignocellulose (c), TEM image of CQDs (d), and the insets in (d) show the corresponding digital photos excited with visible light and UV light.

Structural characteristics of CQD nanoparticles: schematic of CQD (a); FT-IR spectra (b); XPS wide-scan spectrum (c) and the corresponding C1s, N1s, O1s and Mg1s core peaks (d); the inset in (c) shows the atomic percentage.

Fluorescent properties of CQD nanoparticles: Three-dimensional fluorescence mapping (a), Fluorescence spectra (b) and CIE coordinate (c) excited within a wide wavenumber range of 290 ∼ 420 nm, UV–vis absorption spectra (d), (αhν)2 vs hν curve (e), Schematic of the energy transition mechanism of CQDs with different doped types (f).

Digital photos of CQD nanoparticles in aqueous solution under pH = 3–12 excited with visible light (a) and UV light (b); the corresponding fluorescent spectra (c) and fluorescent intensity upon the repeated regulation of pH stimulation (d).

Morphologies and fluorescent properties of CQDs@PVA film: Digital photo (a), The transmittance of pure PVA and CQDs@PVA films (b), SEM images of the cross section (c-d), and the corresponding EDS scanning of C, N, O and Mg elements (e); Three-dimensional fluorescence mapping (f), Fluorescence spectra (g) and CIE coordinate (h) excited within a wide wavenumber range of 290 ∼ 420 nm, Fluorescence quantum yields of CQDs and CQDs@PVA films (i), FT-IR spectra of PVA and CQDs@PVA films (j); Schematic of the hydrogen bonding interaction between CQDs and PVA chains (k).
The stress–strain curve of CQDs@PVA film (a); fluorescent behaviors under different deformation-forms (b-e).

Schematic of wearable pH-detector assembled with CQDs@PVA film (a); Fluorescent spectra of the CQDs@PVA film under pH = 4–8 excited with UV light (b); The corresponding fluorescent intensity upon the repeated regulation of pH stimulation (c), The pH sensing and detection in running processing (d).

head.ce:title: Designing of biomass-derived carbon quantum dots@polyvinyl alcohol film with excellent fluorescent performance and pH-responsiveness for intelligent detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Nonferrous Metals and Featured Materials, School of Resources, Environment and Materials, No. 100, Daxuedong Road, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Nonferrous Metals and Featured Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, The Pennsylvania State University, University Park, PA 16802, USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Nonferrous Metals and Featured Materials, School of Resources, Environment and Materials, No. 100, Daxuedong Road, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Nonferrous Metals and Featured Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted extensive attentions because of their unique luminescent properties and promising prospects in intelligent sensing and detection. However, the increasing environmental pollution caused by chemical substances for synthesizing illuminants limits their expansion in practical applications. Herein, we employed a facile and green synthesis strategy to extract biomass-derived CQDs from natural lignocellulose. The obtained CQD nanoparticles not only showed excellent fluorescent performance, but also posed desirable environmental-friendly characteristic. Moreover, to relieve the fluorescence quenching induced by CQDs aggregation, the well-dispersed CQDs@polyvinyl alcohol (CQDs@PVA) composite film with strong hydrogen-bond interaction was successfully fabricated. The resultant film exhibited high transparency (transmittance of 88%), excellent mechanical flexibility (tensile strength of 39.7 MPa and elongation at break of 453%), and exceptional fluorescence performance. The high concentrated emitting centered in 502 nm emission wavelength was realized in a wide excitation range of 350 ∼ 420 nm with high-stability turquoise fluorescence. More impressively, this fluorescent film demonstrated a sensitive pH-responsiveness, i.e., the fluorescent intensity varied upon pH stimulation. As a proof-of-concept, an intelligent pH-detector based on this fluorescent film was rationally designed for real-time sensing and detection of pH variation in human sweat during movement processing. From fundamental materials to desired applications, this study not only enriches the synthetic strategy of illuminants in an environment-friendly manner, but also paves the way for further applications in wearable, real-time health monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent materials play a significant role in information technologies including marking, transmission and exchange [1–3]. In nature, numerous luminescent organisms, like bacteria, fungi, worms, mollusks, crustaceans, insects, etc., can emit luminescence upon the stimulation of light, electricity, temperature, humidity, and chemical substances [4,5]. Inspired by these natural illuminants, diverse luminescent materials have been developed and applied in various fields, such as intelligent sensing and detection [6], flexible optoelectronic fabrics [7], luminescent devices [8], and drug tracking [9]. However, the increasing environmental pollution caused by chemical substances for synthesizing illuminants is becoming more and more serious in recent years. It is increasingly urgent to figure out alternatives to replace conventional, environment-unfriendly synthetic substances and processing methods.

Lignocellulose derived from nature, with renewable, sustainability, and unique luminescent characteristics, is expected to become an ideal precursor for preparing biomass illuminants [10–12]. Recently, a series of lignocellulose-based fluorescent materials have been successfully designed and assembled [13–15]. Among them, a novel class of lignocellulose-based quantum dots, also known as biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs), has emerged as promising fluorescent nanomaterials due to their excellent luminescent performance [16–19]. For instance, Liu et al., [13] developed a lignin-derived CQDs for real-time and visual self-detection of formaldehyde gas. Yang et al., [20] developed a green method to fabricate sulfur-doped CQDs for Sudan I detection in acidic solution. More interestingly, some CQDs possess a unique pH-responsive characteristic, i.e., the fluorescent intensity and colors would be converted upon the pH stimulation in various value [21]. Typically, Sun et al., [22] synthesized an efficient and pH-sensitive anti-counterfeiting material employed by silicon-doped CQDs, showing convenient information encryption and decryption through acid/alkali treatment. Chang et al., [23] assembled CQDs into pH sensors for both in vivo and in vitro pH detection. This widely broadened the application scenarios in biology and medicine for intelligent sensing and detection of pH variation. The regulating mechanism for this pH-responsiveness mainly attributes to the different ionization degrees of functional groups in CQDs framework upon pH variation, which directly affects the deprotonation degrees, thus resulting in the variation of fluorescent properties [24]. However, limited by the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching of CQDs, the fluorescent stability compromised upon long-term use, thus attenuating the fluorescent properties [25].

Very recently, to relieve fluorescence quenching induced by CQDs aggregation, a novel strategy has been proposed by encapsulating CQD nanoparticles into polymer matrix to restrict their movement for a homogeneous distribution [26,27]. For instance, Qu et al., [28] prepared a uniformly dispersed CQDs@tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) composite xerogel with a high CQDs loading (19.2 wt%) and photoluminescence quantum yields (QYs) over 40%. Jin et al., [29] fabricated CQDs@cellulose powders with uniform distribution and excellent formability through wrapping CQDs with cellulose derivatives. Although incorporating CQDs with polymer matrix could inhibit the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching and render relatively uniform distribution in matrix, some desirable fluorescent properties of CQDs would be compromised, e.g., their sensitivity to pH stimulation as encapsulated in polymer matrix [30]. However, this sensitivity of CQDs is important for applications in medical diagnosis, life science, environmental monitoring, wearable smart devices, etc [22,31]. To the best of our knowledge, there have rarely been studies concentrating on the pH-responsiveness of CQDs-based polymer composites [32]. The intrinsic limitation of the encapsulated system lies in the blocked ionic inside the solid-state CQDs-based polymer composites, which inactivated CQDs from pH stimulation. Therefore, it calls for the development of CQDs-based materials featuring exceptional fluorescent performance and sensitive pH-responsiveness to fully utilize the CQDs in polymer substrate.

In this report, to achieve the desired CQDs-based materials, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was optimally selected as the polymer substrate after evaluating its structure and properties. As is well-known, the abundant hydroxyl groups along PVA chains would provide sufficient sites for hydrogen bonding with CQDs, thus restricting the CQDs movements and realizing the homogeneous dispersion in PVA matrix. Besides, the hydroxyl-enriched chains would lock up a large number of water molecules during solution mixing process and spontaneously build an artificial channel for ion migration, ensuring the sensitive responsiveness of CQDs for pH stimulation [33,34]. With these in mind, the biomass-derived CQDs were first synthesized by using natural lignocellulose, and meanwhile the desired CQDs@PVA film was fabricated via solution casting method. As expected, the well-dispersed CQDs was realized in PVA matrix. Besides, the as-prepared CQDs endowed the composite film with excellent mechanical flexibility, exceptional fluorescent properties and sensitive pH-responsiveness in the giving pH range (4 ∼ 8), thus showing the promising potential in intelligent sensing and detection. Overall, this outstanding breakthrough not only overcomes the issue of aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching of CQDs, but also demonstrates the prospects of CQDs-based composites in intelligent applications.

Natural lignocellulose originated from wood, possesses renewable, sustainability, biodegradability, and nearly pollution-free characteristics, and has been considered as an ideal precursor for the preparation of biomass-derived CQDs due to its enriched aromatic rings, oxygen-rich groups and unsaturated-bond structures [35,36]. Fig. 1 a schematically depicted the preparation procedure of CQD nanoparticles and CQDs@PVA film. To extract lignocellulose, the pristine wood chips (Fig. 1b) with cellular structures were treated with an environmentally-friendly deep eutectic solvent (DES). As expected, the lignocellulose aggregations with a diameter of ∼ 50 nm were obtained (Fig. 1c). Following that, the desired CQDs were synthesized by treating the as-prepared lignocellulose through a hydrothermal reaction. As shown in Fig. 1d, the high-resolution TEM image indicated that the synthesized CQD nanoparticles exhibited a uniform size distribution and high crystallinity with lattice spacing of 0.21 and 0.24 nm, which could be attributed to the (110) and (100) lattice fringes of CQDs framework, respectively [37]. Besides, the carbon skeleton of CQDs was further verified by XRD and Raman spectra (Figure S1) [35,38]. In addition, the fluorescence behaviors of CQDs in aqueous solution under the excitation of visible light and ultraviolet (UV) light were observed in the inset (Fig. 1d). The CQD solution was completely transparent in visible light. In contrast, it demonstrated a strong turquoise fluorescence under UV light, which may be attributed to the successful doping of multi-elements in CQDs framework, thus enriching the surface-states of CQDs and achieving an excellent fluorescent behavior [39,40].

To gain an insight into our CQD nanoparticles, the structural characteristics were explored by FT-IR and XPS analyses. The potential schematic of CQDs was depicted in Fig. 2 a, in which the multi-elements were doped on the surface of CQD framework. To experimentally demonstrate this possible doping, the FT-IR spectra of wood chips, lignocellulose, and CQD nanoparticles were described in Fig. 2b. Clearly, the characteristic absorptions of functional groups in CQDs spectrum were almost the same with that of wood and lignocellulose samples, e.g., the stretching vibrations of O − H groups at 3450 cm−1, C − H groups at 2932 cm−1, and the stretching vibrations of C = C groups at 1633 cm−1. These correlative characteristic peaks confirmed that the structure of aromatic rings was well maintained in CQDs framework. Nevertheless, in contrast to the spectra of wood and lignocellulose, some differences were noticed in CQDs spectrum. Typically, the attenuation of Ar − O groups at 1267 cm−1 illustrated a high graphitization existing in CQDs [41]. Moreover, some novel characteristic peaks were generated at 1334, 817 and 579 cm−1 in CQDs spectrum, indicating that the new interaction was formed in the CQDs framework. Unambiguously, the peaks at 1334 and 817 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of C − N groups, and the peak at 579 cm−1 was corresponded to the bending vibration of metal–ligand [42].

The XPS spectra of CQDs were further verified the interaction between C, N, O and Mg elements. As shown in Fig. 2c, the XPS wide-scan spectrum of CQDs exhibited four characteristic peaks at 284, 399, 531 and 1303 eV, which could be assigned to the C, N, O and Mg elements of CQDs, respectively. The inset displayed the atomic percentage, corresponding to C ∼ 74.8%, N ∼ 3.2%, O ∼ 21.7% and Mg ∼ 0.3%. These successful doping would further enrich the surface states of the CQDs framework, thus endowing them with excellent luminescent performance [39,40]. Detailly, for C1s core peaks (Fig. 2d), there were four peaks at 282.5, 284.0, 285.6, and 288.2 eV, ascribing to the C − Mg, C − C/C = C, C − O/C − N, and C = O bands, respectively. The existing of C − Mg and C − N bands indicated the interaction between carbon skeletal chain and Mg, N elements. The N1s core peaks further proved this point, three types of N doping were observed, including N − Mg (397.3 eV), pyrrolic − N (398.8 eV), and graphitic − N band (401.3 eV). It is worth mentioning that the atomic proportion of pyrrolic-N in N1s spectrum was about to 84.4%, implying that the most of N element in CQDs were present in the form of C − N − C groups. Besides, for the O1s core peaks, apart from the primary peaks of O − C bond (530.9 eV) and O = C bond (531.8 eV), a new peak was observed at 530.2 eV, which could be assigned to the O − Mg bond. More intuitively, the Mg1s core peaks existed three representative peaks at 1302.4, 1303.4, and 1304.4 eV, which were attributed to Mg − C, Mg − N and Mg − O bands, respectively. These peaks in Mg1s spectrum were consistent with the combinations noticed in the above C1s, N1s, and O1s spectra. Therefore, through the structural analysis for CQD nanoparticles, it is fully confirmed that the Mg and N elements were successfully doped on the CQDs framework. For detailed reaction mechanism, the doping of N element is mainly realized through the dehydration and condensation between lignocellulose and ethylenediamine in hydrothermal environment, and the doping of Mg element is yielded from the chelation [38,40].

In order to gain deep insight into the fluorescent properties of CQD nanoparticles, the correlative measurements were carried out and exhibited in Fig. 3 . The three-dimensional fluorescence mapping of CQDs was displayed in Fig. 3a. Apparently, it was focused on a characteristic point, corresponding to a maximum excitation at 410 nm and emission at 518 nm on the fluorescence mapping. For visualized observation, the representative fluorescence spectra were further highlighted in Fig. 3b. Clearly, the fluorescence spectra demonstrated an excitation-dependent behavior, i.e., the emitting center posed a significant red-shift from 404 to 518 nm as the excitation wavelength increased from 290 to 420 nm. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity was accompanied with an increasing tendency. Besides, the corresponding Commission Internationale de L’Eclairage (CIE) coordinate was provided in Fig. 3c. In the excitation range of 290 ∼ 420 nm, the emitting color of CQDs gradually shifted from blue to green, suggesting a weak fluorescence stability for CQDs in solution state.

To gain a better understanding for the energy transition mechanism of Mg and N-doped CQDs, the digital photo and UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs with different doping patterns were shown in Fig. 3d and Figure S2&S3. Typically, a shoulder peak at 270 nm was noticed in CQDs spectrum, which could be assigned to the π → π* transition of aromatic rings in CQDs framework [43]. More interestingly, the CQDs spectrum existed a wide peak at 405 nm, which could be assigned to the unique surface structure of Mg and N-doped CQDs [37]. Moreover, these absorption spectra were converted to Tauc plots, according to the equation:
(1)
α h υ
2
= A
h υ -
E g
where, α, h, ν, A and E g represents for absorption coefficient, Planck constant, light frequency, a constant and excited-state energy band, respectively.

Thereafter, the representative Tauc curve of Mg and N-doped CQDs was shown in Fig. 3e (others provided in Figure S4). The fitting results indicated that the excited-state energy band (E g) of CQDs, N-CQDs, Mg-CQDs, and U-CQDs were corresponded to 2.77, 3.13, 3.32 and 3.55 eV, respectively. Thus, the energy transition mechanism of these CQDs with different doped types was schematically depicted in Fig. 3f. In the excitation of light, electrons (e-) jump from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), accompanying with the formation of the holes (h + ) in the VB. The formed electrons and holes further reach the Eg from their respective CB and VB through relaxation processes and populate an excited state associated with this transition energy. Meanwhile, these particles can be radiatively recombined and emit different photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. Herein, the lower Eg attributed to the successful doping of N and Mg elements in CQDs framework results in the easier radiative-recombination upon the light excitation, thus corresponding to the better fluorescent behavior [37,44].

In addition to exceptional fluorescent performance, pH-responsiveness of CQD nanoparticles was also crucial for its potential applications in intelligent sensing and detection. Hence, the correlative behaviors were evaluated upon different pH stimulation (pH = 3–12). As shown in Fig. 4 a-b, the CQD solution was completely transparent in visible light. Meanwhile, as emitting in UV light, the fluorescent intensity gradually decreased as the pH value altered from 3 to 12, i.e., the highest intensity occurred in acidic environment with pH = 3, whereas the lowest one appeared in alkaline environment with pH = 12. The detail fluorescent intensities were detected under a maximum excitation wavenumber of 410 nm for a quantitative evaluation. As expected, the fluorescent intensity exhibited a remarkable responsiveness upon pH stimulation (Fig. 4c), and a linear downward trend of fluorescence intensity was revealed with pH increase (Figure S5&S6). The main reason for this change could be attributed to the different ionization degrees of functional groups in CQDs framework upon pH variation, which directly affects the deprotonation degrees, thus resulting in the variation of fluorescent properties [24]. The photobleaching resistance of CQDs was also tested under continuous-UV irradiation (Figure S7). The result showed that the maximum fluorescent intensity was almost no change over time in a continuous-UV environment, suggesting a good photobleaching resistance for CQDs nanoparticles.

Whether the fluorescent intensity still maintains the stability during the repeated regulation of pH stimulation? It is meaningful for these pH-responsive CQDs. As designed, the fluorescent intensities of CQDs were detected during the repeated-regulation processing (Fig. 4d). Notably, in multiple circulations, the intensity always kept in a stable region while the pH value was fixed. Through this verification, it can be sure that the CQDs not only exhibited a sensitive pH-responsiveness, but also maintained a steady fluorescent property upon the repeated regulation of pH stimulation. This ideal performance further broadens the prospects of CQDs in the accurate detection for pH variation [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologies and fluorescence properties of CQDs@PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: The above work provided a comprehensive understanding of structures and properties of CQD nanoparticles. However, to achieve the desired application, there is still an intrinsic deficiency to be overcome, i.e., the fluorescence quenching induced by CQDs aggregation in solution state [45]. Based on previously reported work, the encapsulation of CQDs into polymer matrix represents an efficient method to restrict the CQDs movements and render a uniform dispersion [46–48]. With this strategy, the CQDs@PVA film was successfully fabricated and shown in Fig. 5 a. Apparently, it kept a high transparency under visible light. To make a quantitative analysis, the transmittances of pure PVA and CQDs@PVA films were measured in UV–vis wavenumber (200 ∼ 700 nm). As displayed in Fig. 5b, in the low-wavenumber region (UV light), the transmittance of CQDs@PVA film was slightly lower than that of pure PVA. The reason is mainly due to the absorption action of CQD nanoparticles to UV light, which could be demonstrated in Fig. 3d. In contrast, the transmittance of CQDs@PVA film was significantly improved in high-wavenumber region (visible light), reaching about 88%, which was almost the same as that of pure PVA. This high-transmittance performance may be attributed to the uniform distribution of CQD nanoparticles in PVA matrix. The cross section of film was further observed in Fig. 5c. Clearly, a smooth surface was noticed, accompanied with a thickness of 180 μm. In high-magnification SEM image (Fig. 5d), there was almost no agglomerating particle. For observation in a more direct manner, EDS scanning of C, N, O and Mg elements was detected in Fig. 5e. Each element was well-dispersed in the interior of composite film. This homogeneous result may be related to the strong interaction between CQDs and PVA chains. The potential hydrogen bonding interaction was confirmed by FT-IR analysis in below.

The fluorescent properties of CQDs@PVA film were further investigated in Fig. 5f-h. The three-dimensional fluorescence mapping indicated that a high concentrated center occurred in 410 nm excitation and 502 nm emission (Fig. 5f). As compared with CQDs particles (Fig. 3a), this emissive center was blue-shifted from 518 nm after the fabrication of CQDs@PVA film. Unambiguously, it is mainly related to the fluorescence mechanism of CQDs, i.e, the different contribution of surface-state emission. In aqueous solution, the free-movement CQDs tend to aggregate together over time, thus leading to the attenuation of surface-state emission, showing a low-energy and long-wavelength fluorescence. On the contrary, the restrained CQDs originated from the abundant hydrogen bonding between CQDs and PVA chains possess the steady surface-state contribution, corresponding to a high-energy and short-wavelength emission [49]. To highlight this behavior, the representative fluorescence spectra were depicted in Fig. 5g. Similar to the fluorescent characteristics of CQD nanoparticles (Fig. 3b), the fluorescence spectra of CQDs@PVA film exhibited an excitation-dependent behavior, i.e., the fluorescent intensity was increased gradually while the excitation wavelength altered from 290 nm to 420 nm. However, it is worth noting that there was almost no red-shifting phenomenon during the increase of excitation wavelength. The CIE coordinate was further confirmed this feature, where the emitting color always remained a strong turquoise fluorescence within this wide excitation range (Fig. 5h). Hence, it can be confirmed that the CQDs@PVA film exhibited high-stability fluorescence than that of CQDs in solution state. More excitingly, apart from the exceptional fluorescent performance for CQDs@PVA film, it also possessed excellent fluorescence quantum yields (QYs), corresponding to 18.67% (Fig. 5i), which was 6.7 times higher than that of CQDs in aqueous solution (2.78%). As a result, the introduction of PVA not only provided a loading substrate for CQDs, but also served sufficient guarantee for excellent fluorescent properties, e.g., high-stability fluorescence and high QYs. The reason for this is attributed to the strong hydrogen bonding between CQDs and PVA chains. To verify our hypothesis, the FT-IR spectra of pure PVA and CQDs@PVA films was displayed in Fig. 5j. Clearly, the characteristic absorptions of functional groups of PVA-based films were almost the same. However, after incorporating CQDs, the featured absorption peaks of the stretching vibration and bending vibration of O − H group were shifted from 3453 to 3432 cm−1 and from 1441 to 1419 cm−1, respectively. This significant blue-shift implied the formation of the new hydrogen bonding between CQDs and PVA chains, thus relieving the fluorescence quenching induced by CQDs aggregation and rendering the ideal fluorescent behaviors for CQDs@PVA film. Moreover, the corresponding mechanism of hydrogen bonding interaction was vividly described in Fig. 5k.

In addition to the ideal fluorescence performance, excellent mechanical properties of CQDs@PVA film are of great importance for practical applications. To gain a deep insight into the mechanical behaviors of CQDs@PVA film, the representative stress–strain curve and the corresponding fluorescent spectra under different stretching ratios was shown in Fig. 6 a & Figure S8. The film possessed an outstanding mechanical performance, where the maximum tensile strength reached 37.9 MPa, far beyond the common-used polymeric materials. Besides, it posed a well mechanical flexibility, corresponding to an elongation at break of 453%. Obviously, the above results suggested that the CQDs@PVA composites still kept excellent mechanical behaviors even incorporating with CQD nanoparticles. Additionally, the corresponding fluorescent spectra revealed that this film always maintained a steady fluorescent behavior even with different stretching ratios. Moreover, the different deformation-forms were also carried out in Fig. 6b-e. Impressively, no matter bending, rolling, twisting, or folding, there was almost no structural or fluorescent defect in the surface of CQDs@PVA film. These behaviors indicated that the resultant film possessed excellent mechanical properties and steady fluorescent behaviors even in different deformation-forms, which is crucial for the application in wearable fluorescent devices [49,50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wearable pH-detector assembled with CQDs@PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: The as-designed CQDs@PVA film featuring high-stability fluorescence and mechanical properties poses a promising potential to assemble diverse devices for a huge array of prospective applications. As a concept, a wearable pH-detector assembled with this film was innovatively proposed and designed to serve human health. As is well-known, human sweat has a strict pH distribution between 4 and 8, which is driven by lactic acid secreted by exercising muscles [51]. Hence, the intelligent detection for pH variation is of great significance to reflect the physiological information of human health [52,53]. As a proof-of-concept, the wearable pH-detector was schematically depicted in Fig. 7 a. It was mainly composed of UV-light emitter and collector, and pH-detection module (pH-responsive film). Before application, the pH-responsiveness of this CQDs@PVA film was first evaluated. The fluorescent spectra of film were detected upon different pH stimulation (Fig. 7b). As expected, the fluorescent intensity decreased with the increase of pH value, showing the desired pH-responsiveness in giving range of human sweat. It is worth mentioning that although the CQD nanoparticles were encapsulated in PVA matrix, the hydroxyl-enriched PVA chains could stabilize large amount of water molecules, which contributed to establish an artificial ion-migration channel for the rapid movement of various components in human sweat, thus resulting in the composite film with highly sensitive responsiveness upon pH stimulation. The fluorescent stability was also crucial for this pH-responsive film. Hence, the intensity was further evaluated in the repeated regulation of pH stimulation. As shown in Fig. 7c, their intensities were always kept in a stable region as the pH value fixed, suggesting an accurate and stable detection of this film for pH stimulation. This outstanding behavior laid a solid foundation for our designed pH-detector. Thereafter, the direct evaluation of pH variation reflected by fluorescent intensity was conducted in practical running processing. As displayed in Fig. 7d, the real-time detection of pH variation in human sweat were successfully collected by the tightly skin-fitted CQDs@PVA film and the relative intensity of fluorescence was increased with the increase of running time, implying the decrease of pH value accompanied with the secretion of sweat. After operation for 30 mins, the ratio of relative intensity was up to 1.48, suggesting the significant variation existed in this running processing. Thus, it is confident that this wearable pH-detector has huge potential to accurately sense and detect pH variation in human sweat during movement processing. As a consequence, we expect that this high-performance and pH-responsive film could be applied to wearable intelligent devices in the near future and made better services for human health.

In summary, an environment-friendly strategy was developed for synthesizing biomass-derived CQDs from natural lignocellulose via hydrothermal treatment. The as-prepared CQDs were successfully doped with N and Mg elements onto their framework, showing strong turquoise fluorescence. The desired CQDs@PVA fluorescent film was further fabricated by a solution casting method. A uniform distribution of CQDs in PVA matrix has been realized due to the hydrogen bonding interaction between CQDs and PVA chains. The resultant film exhibited high transmittance of 88% in visible light as well as excellent mechanical flexibility with 39.7 MPa tensile strength and 453% elongation at break. Moreover, the CQDs endowed the film with exceptional fluorescent performance. It possessed a high-stability turquoise fluorescence in a wide excitation wavenumber (350 ∼ 420 nm) and high quantum yields of 18.67%, which is 670% higher than that of CQDs in aqueous solution (2.78%). Notably, the fluorescent intensity of film was sensitive upon pH stimulation. As a proof-of-concept, an intelligent pH-detector based on this pH-responsive film was rationally designed for real-time sensing and detection of pH variation in human sweat during movement processing, so as to achieve better services for human health. Overall, this work not only develops an environment-friendly route for the fabrication of high-stability and strong fluorescent materials, but also opens up broad prospects in the accurate sensing and detection of wearable intelligent devices.

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) with an average polymerization degree of 1700 ± 50 and alcoholysis degree of 99%, was supported by SINOPEC Sichuan Vinylon Works, China. Pristine wood was obtained from Nanning Diwang Village Wood Industry Company, China. Choline chloride, oxalic acid, magnesium hydroxide, ethylenediamine, and glycerol were provided by Macklin Co., Ltd, China. The dialysis tubing (MWCO 500–1000 Da) was purchased from Yuanyebio Co., Ltd, China. Ethanol was supplied by Jinan Hanbai Chem Co., Ltd, China. All materials were used without further purification.

For the extraction of lignocellulose, 0.5 mol choline chloride and 0.5 mol oxalic acid were first mixed in a 500 mL beaker and then stirred violently for 1 h at 80 °C. After the formation of a homogeneous DES, 4 g wood chips collected from pristine wood were added and continuously stirred for another 8 h at 110 °C, then filtered to obtain the lignocellulose-enriched DES solution (It is worth noted that the reaction conditions selected in here were optimized through our pre-experiment, and the detailed descriptions were displayed in Table S1). Subsequently, the as-prepared solution was dumped into 1 L ethanol/water anti-solvent with a molar ratio of 1:10, and stirred overnight to extract lignocellulose. Thereafter, the mixtures were filtrated, washed and freeze-dried to obtain the desired lignocellulose nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQD nanoparticles and CQDs@PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 0.5 g extracted lignocellulose was added into 10 mL ultra-pure deionized water and ultrasonic-dispersed for 10 min. After the formation of a suspension, 8.5 mmol magnesium hydroxide (magnesium source) and 15 mmol ethylenediamine (nitrogen source) were slowly added and violently stirred for 1 h. Subsequently, the suspension was moved to a 25 mL PPL-lined autoclave and heated at 255 °C for 10 h. The as-prepared mixtures were cooled down to room temperature, placed for another 2 h, and then filtered through a membrane with 220 nm pore. The obtained filtrate was transferred to dialysis tubing (MWCO 500–1000 Da) under ultra-pure deionized water treatment for 3 days. Finally, the purified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were obtained by freeze-drying. Here, compared with most of previously reported CQDs, our fabrication for biomass-derived CQDs is relatively environmentally-friendly although limited portion of magnesium hydroxide and ethylenediamine [54]. Following that, 0.1 g purified CQDs were added into 100 mL ultra-pure deionized water under mechanical stirring and ultrasonic dispersion for 30 min. Then, 10 g PVA particles was added into the above dispersion and violently stirred for 4 h at 80 °C. After fully dissolution, the blending was transferred to a culture dish, and then dried at 40 °C for 2 days to obtain the desired CQDs@PVA film.

The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of CQDs and CQDs@PVA composites was analyzed by a Fourier spectrometer (Nicolet iS 50, Thermo, USA) in the wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectra of CQDs were collected on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy spectrometer (K-Alpha, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The morphology of CQDs was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-2800, JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The cross-sectional microscopy and elemental analysis of CQDs@PVA film were measured by field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Sigma 300, Zeiss, Germany). The Raman spectrum of CQDs was tested by a laser micro-Raman spectrometer (inVia Reflex, Renishaw, UK). The XRD spectrum of CQDs was tested by a D8 Discover X-ray Diffractometer (A24A10, BRUKER AXS GMBH, Germany). The UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs and CQDs@PVA film were detected on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence spectra of CQDs and CQDs@PVA film were collected on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). The fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) of CQDs and CQDs@PVA film were tested by Transient steady state spectroscopy (FLS1000, Edinburgh Instruments, UK) associated with a photometric integrating sphere assisted method (Formula S1).
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Innovation-Driven Project Funds of Guangxi (AA17204087-16).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.136442.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
